coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effect of confinement and photocatalysis-endowed adjustable peroxymonosulfate activation pathway to degrade antibiotics

coredata.dc:description: This study firstly reported the regulation of PMS activation pathway via the novel catalyst (CQDs/Co@CN) that contained carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and confined nano Co dual active sites. CQDs/Co@CN showed superior catalytic activity for PMS activation due to the synergistic effect of CQDs and confined nano Co, and sulfamethoxazole degradation rate reached 0.661 min−1, which was even higher than that obtained by single atom catalyst. The reactive species in the system of CQDs/Co@CN/PMS could be easily regulated by the visible light. In the absence of visible light, PMS was mainly activated to produce Co(IV) = O, while it was activated to simultaneously produce radicals and Co(IV) = O in the presence of visible light. Moreover, the yields of Co(IV) = O increased by about 10 %. Experimental results and DFT calculation demonstrated that CQDs played crucial role in the regulation of reactive species. This study could provide a way to modulate the reactive species by visible light.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effect of confinement and photocatalysis-endowed adjustable peroxymonosulfate activation pathway to degrade antibiotics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study firstly reported the regulation of PMS activation pathway via the novel catalyst (CQDs/Co@CN) that contained carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and confined nano Co dual active sites. CQDs/Co@CN showed superior catalytic activity for PMS activation due to the synergistic effect of CQDs and confined nano Co, and sulfamethoxazole degradation rate reached 0.661 min−1, which was even higher than that obtained by single atom catalyst. The reactive species in the system of CQDs/Co@CN/PMS could be easily regulated by the visible light. In the absence of visible light, PMS was mainly activated to produce Co(IV) = O, while it was activated to simultaneously produce radicals and Co(IV) = O in the presence of visible light. Moreover, the yields of Co(IV) = O increased by about 10 %. Experimental results and DFT calculation demonstrated that CQDs played crucial role in the regulation of reactive species. This study could provide a way to modulate the reactive species by visible light.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Peroxymonosulfate (PMS)-based advanced oxidation process has been widely investigated for the removal of emerging pollutants in the water and wastewater due to its own advantages such as easy storage and activation [1–3]. To now PMS activation can be classified by homogeneous activation such as ultrasound and heterogeneous activation [4–6]. For heterogeneous activation, heterogeneous catalysts played crucial role in the PMS-based advanced oxidation process [7,8]. The different structures of catalyst could result in different PMS activation pathway. For instance, ball milling treatment of Mn3O4 and Mn3O4@β-cyclodextrin could efficiently regulate electron transfer pathway for PMS activation [9,10]. Nevertheless, the high catalytic activity and stability is highly desirable characteristics for heterogeneous catalysts. Furthermore, due to the complex water matrices, the regulation of reactive species in the PMS-based advanced oxidation process for constructing the selective oxidation system is necessary for efficient removal of emerging pollutants [11].

Previous studies have demonstrated that the regulation of coordination structure of active sites such as single atom metal active sites could control the catalytic mechanism for PMS that regulated the formation of reactive species[12–14]. But the modulation of coordination structure of active sites usually required high temperature treatment, and how to precisely modulate the coordination structure still remains challenging.

In addition to the regulation of coordination structure, the addition of active site could also increase the catalytic activity and regulate the reactive species. Our previous study has demonstrated that compared to single atom Fe catalyst, the catalyst with single atom Fe and nano Fe2N dual active sites showed better catalytic activity and stability [15]. Furthermore, the catalyst with dual active sites induced different reactive species compared with the catalyst with single active site [16,17]. But for the above dual active sites’ catalyst, the limited single atom metal active sites and the direct exposure of active site in solution definitely decrease the catalytic stability in the practical application. In contrast, transition metal-encapsulated carbon nanotubes (M@CN) endowed the transition metal high stability due to the wrapping structure [18]. Moreover, the confinement could endow the transition metal new catalytic characteristic [19]. But how to increase the catalytic activity of M@CN and regulate the reactive species in the system of M@CN/PMS is still a challenge.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been demonstrated to be capable of activating PMS [20]. Moreover, CQDs possess photocatalytic activity [21,22]. The photo-induced electron by CQDs may promote the regeneration of the active sites and regulate the PMS activation pathway [23]. It is thus speculated that the carbon tubes supported CQDs and encapsulated nano Co dual active sites catalyst (CQDs/Co@NC) may possess superior catalytic activity and stability. However, to now, there was no study to investigate the synergistic effect of the confinement and photocatalysis on the removal of emerging pollutants such as antibiotics from the complex water matrices. The synergistic mechanism of the confinement and photocatalysis has not been disclosed.

Therefore, this study aimed to prepare the CQDs/Co@NC and investigate the catalytic activity of CQDs/Co@NC for PMS towards the degradation of sulfamethoxazole (SMX) due to its ubiquitous presence [24], to disclose the synergistic mechanism of the confinement and photocatalysis, and to explore the potential way to regulate the types and concentration reactive species.

The following chemicals were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.: urea (98 %), sulfamethoxazole (SMX, 98 %), norfloxacin (NOR, 98 %), citric acid (10 % (w/w)), dialysis bag (1000 mw) and PMS (OXONE). The following chemicals were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co.: methanol (99.5 %), ethanol (99.7 %), p-benzoquinone (99.5 %), sodium chloride (99.5 %), sodium bicarbonate (≥99.5 %), EDTA solution, cobalt nitrate (99 %), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (99 %), sodium hydroxide (95 %) and sulfuric acid (Beijing Lanyi Chemical Products Co. LTD).

Co@NC was synthesized as described in the previous study with slight modification. Firstly, chitosan (10 g), EDTA (3 g), cobalt nitrate (5.4 g) and zinc nitrate hexahydrate (4.16 g) were mixed in 500 ml deionized water. The mixed solution was dispersed by ultrasound for 10 min followed by stir for 1 h. The mixed solution was then dried using a hot plate at 80 °C for 12 h. Thereafter, the above dried solid (0.5 g) and melamine (5 g) were placed in a mortar and ground. The mixed solid was transferred into a tube furnace. The furnace was heated to 550 °C at 10 °C/min for 1 h and then increased to 850 °C/min at 10 °C/min for 1 h. After cooling naturally, the obtained solid was denoted as Co@NC.

The carbon quantum solution was prepared using the following method as described previously [25]. Briefly, citric acid (6 g) and urea (3 g) were dissolved in the deionized water (50 ml). The mixed solution was placed in a 100-mL Teflon stainless autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 180 °C and remained for 5 h. Then the autoclave was naturally cooled. The obtained solution was filtered by 0.22 μm polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) filter and then centrifuged at 12000 rpm/min for 15 min. The supernatant was collected and dialyzed using dialysis bag (1000da) for 48 h. The deionized water outside of the dialysis bag was changed using the same volume of deionized water every 6 h. Finally, the inside solution of dialysis bag was carbon quantum dots solution that was kept at 4 °C for further use.

The CQDs/Co@NC was prepared using the following method. The Co@NC (10 mg) was added into quantitative volume of carbon quantum dots solution and then stirred from 2 h. Thereafter, the mixed solution was freezing dried and then transferred into a tube furnace. The tube furnace was heated to 200 °C at 10 °C/min under nitrogen gas atmosphere and maintained for 1 h. After naturally cooling, the obtained solid was denoted as CQDs/Co@NC.

The following techniques were used to characterize CQDs/Co@NC including BET, transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectrum and X-ray absorption fine structure (XAFS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of CQDs/Co-NC for PMS
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of CQDs/Co@NC was investigated by conducting the SMX degradation experiments in the presence of PMS and visible light using photocatalytic reactor (5 W, PCX50C discover, PerfectLight). Specifically, quantified SMX stock solution was mixed with the deionized water achieving 10 mg/L of SMX concentration. Then quantitative PMS stock solution was added into the SMX solution. The experiments were initiated by adding quantitative CQDs/Co@NC. The whole experiments lasted for 30 min. At interval time, the samples were taken, filtered by 0.45 μm PTFE membrane and quenched immediately with saturated sodium thiosulfate. To investigate the influential factors including inorganic ions, pH, PMS concentration and water components, the experimental conditions were adjusted accordingly.

The SMX concentration was determined using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent 1200 s), and the degradation intermediates products were analyzed using LC-MS (LC/MSD iQ). The PMS concentration was measured using spectrophotometry, and the capacity of electron transfer of CQDs/Co@NC was analyzed using electrochemical methods. The detailed test conditions have been reported in our previous studies [26,27]. The detailed computational methods were shown in the Text S1. The PMS utilization efficiency was calculated based on the molar ratio of consumed PMS to degraded SMX. All the data used in this study were the average of triplicate.

Fig. 1
A showed the SMX degradation in the presence of different catalysts. Compared to Co@NC, CQDs/Co@NC showed different catalytic activity with the additive amount of CQDs. The catalytic activity of CQDs/Co@NC increased with the additive volume of CQDs ranging from 1 to 5 ml among which CQDs/Co@NC-5 induced faster SMX degradation than Co@NC, indicating that there was synergistic effect between Co@NC and CQDs. When the additive volume of CQDs was further increased to 10 ml, the SMX degradation rate obviously decreased, which could be due to the enhanced recombination of electron and hole [28].

The SMX degradation was further simulated by the pseudo first order kinetics as shown in Fig. 1 B. The pseudo first order kinetic constant (k) in the presence of CQDs/Co@NC-5 reached 0.661 min−1 that is 1.42 times higher than that in the presence of Co@NC. While the k in the presence of CQDs/Co@NC-1, CQDs/Co@NC-2 and CQDs/Co@NC-10 was lower than that in the presence of Co@NC. Screening the published references, the catalytic activity of CQDs/Co@NC-5 for PMS was comparable and even better than single atom catalysts ( Table S1 ), further proving the superior catalytic activity of CQDs/Co@NC-5.

In addition to the removal efficiency of SMX, SMX mineralization was also characterized by the removal of total organic carbon (Fig. 1 C). The trend of TOC removal had the same trend with the SMX removal efficiency. CQDs/Co@NC-5 induced the maximum TOC mineralization (46.1 %), followed by Co@NC (34.7 %), CQDs/Co@NC-2 (32.6 %), CQDs/Co@NC-1 (27.5 %) and CQDs/Co@NC-10 (26.4 %).

To investigate the effect of COQs on the catalytic activity, the PMS decomposition along with reaction time was determined as shown in Fig. 1 D. Co@NC induced the fastest decomposition of PMS in which 93.5 % of the initially applied PMS was decomposed at 30 min. The CQDs decoration decreased the PMS decomposition efficiency and followed the order of CQDs/Co@NC-5 > CQDs/Co@NC-2 > CQDs/Co@NC-1 > CQDs/Co@NC-10. Although the final decomposition efficiency for CQDs/Co@NC-2, CQDs/Co@NC-1 and CQDs/Co@NC-10 were almost the same, the PMS decomposition kinetics in the first 10 min presented significant difference. Considering that SMX degradation mainly occurred in the first 10 min, it is thus concluded that CQDs doping affected the PMS activation pathway. In addition, it is noted that the PMS decomposition efficiency induced by CQDs/Co@NC-5 was lower than that induced by Co@NC, but CQDs/Co@NC-5 showed better catalytic activity than Co@NC, suggesting that Co@NC and CQDs/Co@NC-5 induced different reactive species. The PMS utilization efficiency for CQDs/Co@NC-5 was determined as 8.64, which is higher than that reported in the previous study [29,30], further confirming the superior catalytic activity of CQDs/Co@NC-5.

The CQDs/Co@NC-5 was characterized in detail because it had the best catalytic activity for PMS. Fig. 2 showed the XPS spectrums of CQDs/Co@NC-5. It contained carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and cobalt elements with the atomic percentage of 78.74 %, 7.12 %, 12.95 % and 1.19 %, respectively. Carbon was divided into five components (Fig. 2 A): C-C/C = C (284.8 eV), C-O (286.0 eV), C-N (287.5 eV), C = O (288.8 eV) and π* (290.7 eV) [15,31]. For N1s, it contained three components (Fig. 2 B): pyridinic N (398.7 eV), pyrrolic N (400.2 eV) and graphitic N (401.3 eV) [32,33]. For O1s, it also contained three components (Fig. 2C): C = O (531.6 eV), C-O (532.5 eV) and adsorbed O (533.7 eV) [34,35]. For Co2p, it could be divided into six components (Fig. 2 D): Co (778.7 eV), Co(II) (781.4 eV) and satellite peaks (785.0, 787.4, 796.7 and 802.7 eV) [36–38].

XRD was used to characterize the crystal structure of CQDs/Co@NC-5. The peaks appearing at 24.6° corresponded to the facet of (002) facet of graphitic carbon, while the peak appearing at 26.6° was ascribed to the facet of (004) of CQDs [39,40] (Fig. 2 E), while no peak at 26.6° was found for Co@NC in the XRD figures (Fig. 2 E), demonstrating the successful decoration of CQDs.

Raman spectrum of CQDs/Co@NC-5 clearly showed three characteristic peaks located at 1347.1, 1591.2 and 2693.5 cm−1 (Fig. 2 F), respectively, which corresponded to the D band, G band and 2D band. The ratio of ID to IG was usually represented as the defective degree of graphitic carbon. It was determined as 1.09, while the ratio for Co@NC was 1.07 (Fig. 2 F), indicating that CQDs/Co@NC-5 had high defective degree, which could be due to the defects in the nitrogen-CQDs or interfacial defects between CQDs and graphitic carbon [41,42].

TEM was used to observe the morphology of CQDs/Co@NC-5. In the absence of CQDs, the carbon nanotube structure was clearly found. Inside the carbon nanotube, a black dot with the lattice spacing of 0.20 nm was observed ( Fig. S1 ), which is attributed to the (111) facet of Co [43]. Moreover, the size of Co was on the nanoscale. In the presence of CQDs, in addition to carbon nanotube and nano Co, another black dot with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was found (Fig. 2 G), indicating the presence of CQDs [44]. It is thus confirmed the successful decoration of CQDs on the Co@NC-5.

XANES was further used to elucidate the coordination structure of Co as shown in Fig. 3 . The adsorption edge of CQDs@NC-5 and Co@NC shifted to higher energy level compared to that of Co foil and to lower energy level compared to that of CoO (Fig. 3 A), suggesting that the Co valence in the CQDs@NC-5 and Co@NC was between 0 and 2. Compared to Co@NC, the adsorption edge of CQDs@NC-5 slightly shifted to a lower energy level, indicating that the Co in the CQDs@NC-5 possessed lower valence that may provide more electrons for PMS activation.

For both CQDs@NC-5 and Co@NC, a significant peak at 2.11 Å was found (Fig. 3 B), which was slightly lower than Co-Co bond (2.18 Å for Co foil). This could be due to the formation of Co2-N coordination structure or shortened Co-Co bond [45,46]. High-resolution wavelet transform (WT) EXAFS analysis was performed in k and R spaces. For both CQDs@NC-5 and Co@NC, the contour plots presented a WT-maximum at 7.1 Å and another WT-maximum at 6.8 Å (Fig. 3 C and D). Both of them indicates the Co-Co bond [47,48], which further suggested that there were two kinds of Co-Co.

BET was further used to characterize the CQDs/Co@NC-5. The N2 adsorption curve followed H3 hysteresis loop ( Fig. S2
A), indicating the presence of irregular mesoporous [49,50]. The pore distribution was depicted in Fig. S2
B. The highest peak appeared at 3.8 nm that could be a fake peak [51]. Another high peak appeared at 3.1 nm, confirming the presence of mesoporous. The average pore diameter was determined as 4.8 nm. In comparison, the N2 adsorption curve of Co@NC had H3 hysteresis loop with the presence of mesoporous ( Fig. S3
A and B). The average pore diameter was the same with CQDs/Co@NC-5. The specific surface area was calculated as 132.5 and 114.9 cm3/g for Co@NC and CQDs/Co@NC-5, respectively, indicating that the decoration of CQDs resulted in the slight decrease of surface area. Based on the above characterization, it is concluded that CQDs doping had no effect on the coordination structure of cobalt, but the surface area and defective degree by tightly coupled with Co@NC.

Quenching experiments and EPR techniques were used to identify the reactive species in the system of CQDs/Co@NC-5/PMS. In the presence of tertiary butanol that is usually considered as the scavenger of HO• [52], the degradation kinetics was slightly inhibited. The final removal efficiency of SMX decreased by 3.4 %, suggesting the minor contribution of HO•. While in the presence of ethanol that is usually used as the scavenger of both HO• and SO4•- [52], the degradation kinetics was significantly depressed and the final removal efficiency decreased by 22.4 % (Fig. 4
A), indicating that SO4•- made more contribution to SMX degradation compared to HO•. EPR results found the characteristic signal of HO• (Fig. 4 B), but not the characteristic signal of SO4•-. Two reasons could be used to explain the phenomenon. The first could be due to the fast transformation of SO4•- to HO• [53]. The second could be due to the fact that HO• was derived from water molecule not from PMS [54].

The addition of furfuryl alcohol inhibited the SMX degradation, and the removal efficiency decreased by 17.7 %. Its inhibition degree was between the tertiary butanol and ethanol. Furfuryl alcohol was usually used as the scavenger of singlet oxygen [55], but it could also react with HO• and SO4•− [56,57].Thus, its inhibition could be due to its scavenger for singlet oxygen, HO• and SO4•-. EPR spectra showed the significant characteristic of singlet oxygen (Fig. 4 C). Moreover, its intensity increased with time, confirming the occurrence of singlet oxygen. Considering the low reaction rate of singlet oxygen with SMX [58], it cannot be concluded that singlet oxygen made major contribution for SMX degradation, although the singlet oxygen was observed with strong intensity.

Potassium dichromate was usually used as the scavenger of electron [59]. In the presence of potassium dichromate, the degradation kinetics of SMX was inhibited, but the final removal efficiency still achieved 100 % (Fig. 4 A), suggesting the contribution of photo-induced electron. In addition, the contribution of Co(IV) = O was also determined using PMSO as probe compounds [60]. The PMSO degraded along with time. Meanwhile, the PMSO2 concentration increased along with the PMSO degradation (Fig. 4 D). Based on the concentration of PMSO and PMSO2, about 90 % of PMSO was converted into PMSO2, indicating that Co(IV) = O made high contribution to the degradation of PMSO.

In general, there were several reactive species including HO•, SO4•-, Co(IV) = O, singlet oxygen and photo-induced electron that were involved in the SMX degradation in which SO4•- and high valence cobalt-oxo made major contributions.

For Co encapsulated in carbon tubes, the produced reactive species varied. For instance, Cheng et al. reported that Co@CN activated PMS to produce SO4•- and 1O2 as main reactive species[73], while Yao et al. reported Co@CN activated PMS to produce SO4•- and HO• as the main reactive species [74]. In our previous study, Co@CN activated PMS to produce radicals and surface-bound reactive species as the main reactive species [75]. In this study, CQDs/Co@CN-5 activated PMS to produce radicals, singlet oxygen and Co(IV) = O. This discrepancy between this study and the previous studies might be due to the CQDs.

To elucidate the role of CQDs in PMS activation, the reactive species in the Co@NC/PMS system was firstly investigated. The results were depicted in Fig. S7 . The SMX removal efficiency decreased by 8.4 % and 3.3 % ( Fig. S7
A), respectively after the addition of ethanol and tertiary butanol. EPR spectra had no characteristics of HO• and SO4•- ( Fig. S7
B), suggesting the minor contribution of radicals for SMX degradation that is different with that in the system of CQDs/Co@CN-5/PMS. The addition of furfuryl alcohol showed lower inhibition on the SMX degradation compared to that in the system of CQDs/Co@CN-5/PMS. EPR spectra further demonstrated the occurrence of singlet oxygen ( Fig. S7
C).

Furthermore, the contribution of Co(IV) = O was also determined as shown in
Fig. S7
D. The formation of PMSO2 along with the PMSO degradation indicates the occurrence of Co(IV) = O. Based on the concentration of PMSO and PMSO2, it is concluded that 80.7 % of PMSO was converted into PMSO2, which is lower than that in the system of CQDs/Co@CN-5. Based on the above contrastive analysis, it is thus concluded that the CQDs doping promoted the formation of radicals, singlet oxygen and Co(IV) = O.

It is noted that CQDs/Co@NC-5 possessed higher PMS utilization efficiency than Co@NC, although the PMS decomposition efficiency in the presence of CQDs/Co@NC-5 was lower than that in the presence of Co@NC, suggesting the different activation pathway induced by CQDs/Co@NC-5 compared to Co@NC. Considering that the difference between CQDs/Co@NC-5 and Co@NC, it is confirmed that the discrepancy was due to the CQDs.

To further disclosure the effect of CQDs decoration, the DFT calculation was employed. The optimized configuration of CQDs/Co@CN-5 was presented in the Fig. S8 . Firstly, the adsorption isothermal curve was simulated for Co@NC-5 and CODs@Co@NC-5. Compared to Co@NC-5, CQDs@Co@NC-5 had high PMS loading (Fig. 5
A). Furthermore, CQDs/Co@NC-5 possessed higher adsorption energy than Co@NC-5 (Fig. 5 B and C), confirming that CQDs decoration increased the adsorption capacity for PMS.

The Co had rich electric charge density as shown in Fig. S8 , which is consistent with the widely accepted opinion that Co was the main active site for PMS activation [76]. To initiate the PMS activation, the Co would be firstly oxidized to produce Co(II). In general, there are two pathways for the formation of Co(II) as shown in R1 and R2. Then, Co(II) would form CoOH+ that was considered as key step for PMS activation (R3) [77,78]. Thereafter, CoOH+ could activate PMS to produce radicals or high-valent cobalt-oxo (R4 and R5). To check the energy barrier for these reactions, the enthalpy change of reactions were determined.
(R1) Co + HSO5 − + H+ → Co2+ + SO4•- + H2O
(R2) Co + O2 + 2H+ → Co2+ + H2O2
(R3) Co2+ + H2O → CoOH+ + H+
(R4) CoOH+ + HSO5 − → CoO+ + SO4•-+H2O
(R5) CoOH+ + HSO5 − → Co(IV) = O2+ + SO4 2- + H2O

For the production of Co(II), CQDs/Co@CN-5 required slightly slower enthalpy changes of R1 and R2 than Co@CN-5 (Fig. 5 D), indicating that CQDs decoration slightly increased the energy barrier. The enthalpy changes of R2 was much higher than that of R1, demonstrating that the oxygen gas in solution is easier to induce the formation of Co(II). The enthalpy change was positive value for R3. The enthalpy changes of CQDs/Co@CN-5 was lower than Co@CN-5, suggesting that CQDs/Co@CN-5 was easier to produce CoOH+.

In addition, the signal of hydroxyl radicals in the presence of CQDs/Co@NC-5 was found without PMS in solution ( Fig. S10 ), confirming that the formation of hydroxyl radicals was mainly due to the electron hole. Meanwhile, the photo-induced electron could activate PMS to produce SO4•-, which could explain the increased contribution of radicals to the SMX degradation. According to the above results, the PMS activation mechanism was proposed as follows: 1) Co nanoparticles was oxidized to produce Co2+ that subsequently interacted with PMS to produce radicals (minor) and high-valent cobalt-oxo (major); 2) visible light-induced CQDs to produce electron hole and electron; the electron hole further interacted with H2O to produce HO• and the electron activated PMS to produce SO4•-. The formation of 1O2 could be due to the interaction between the oxygen-containing functional groups and PMS [79], and the CQDs enhanced the electron transfer further promoting the formation of 1O2. The proposed activation mechanism was depicted in Fig. 5 E.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CQDs/Co@NC-5/PMS under different conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the feasibility of the system of CQDs/Co@NC-5/PMS, the SMX degradation experiments were conducted under different conditions. Solution initial pH showed distinguished effect on the SMX degradation ( Fig. S13
A). The SMX degradation rate increased when the solution pH increased from 3.0 to 5.0. The pHzpc of CQDs/Co@NC-5 was determined as 3.1 ( Fig. S14 ). When the solution pH was lower than 3.1, the surface of CQDs/Co@NC-5 was positively charged, while the surface of CQDs/Co@NC-5 was negatively charged when the solution pH was higher than 3.1.

The different catalytic activity at pH 3.0 and pH 5.0 could be due to the change of surface charge that affected the SMX adsorption and PMS adsorption. When the solution was further increased to 6.7, the SMX degradation decreased that could be due to the decreased concentration of the formed Co(IV) = O [80]. When the solution pH was further increased to 9.0, the SMX degradation rate increased again that could be due to the fact that alkali condition promoted the conversion of SO4•- to HO• [81] or the PMS was activated to produce radicals under alkali condition [82]. In general, the solution pH has less influence on the CQDs/Co@NC-5/PMS system compared to that reported in the previous study [83].

Inorganic ions exhibited different effect on the SMX degradation ( Fig. S13
B). Chloridion showed inhibition on the SMX removal, which is consistent with the previous studies [84]. This could be due to the direct reaction between PMS and chloridion to produce hydrochlorite acid that had low reaction rate with SMX compared to radicals and Co(IV) = O 50. In contrast, nitrate ions slightly accelerated the SMX degradation, which could be due to the formation of active sites induced by nitrate ions [85,86]. Previous study indicated that carbonate ions promoted the SMX degradation [87], while bicarbonate ions significantly inhibited the SMX degradation in this study. The discrepancy could be due to the following reasons. First, 10 mM of bicarbonate ions could result in the solution pH of 8.5 in which bicarbonate ions dominated [56]. Bicarbonate ions can react with radicals to produce bicarbonate radicals that has lower oxidation capacity than carbonate radicals. Secondly, it could be due to the scavenger effect of bicarbonate ions on the photo-induced electron [88]. Thirdly, bicarbonate ions affected the adsorption of PMS and SMX on the surface of CQDs/Co@CN-5 [89].

The increase of PMS initial concentration increased the SMX degradation that could be due to the increased concentration of reactive species ( Fig. S13
C). The water component also had effect on the SMX degradation ( Fig. S13
D). The characteristics of water components was listed in Table S3 . The SMX degradation in the secondary effluents was obviously slower than that in the groundwater and surface water, which could be due to the higher concentration of organic pollutants in the secondary effluents than in the groundwater and surface water.

The continuous CQDs/Co@NC-5/PMS system was constructed to investigate its performance in the removal of antibiotics in the municipal secondary effluents (Fig. 6
A and B). Prior to the degradation experiments, the quantified SMX and NOF solution were added into the municipal secondary effluents separately to achieve the initial concentration of 2 mg/L. The basic characteristics of the secondary effluents were listed in Table S3 . The system was operated intermittently. Each time can treat 200 ml wastewater with the reaction time of 15 min. It can be seen that in the first five runs, the SMX could be completely removed. The SMX removal efficiency slightly decreased to 97.4 % and 91.3 %, respectively in the six and seventh runs. While the NOR could be completely removed in the first four runs, its removal efficiency slightly decreased to 98.3 %, 95.4 % and 94.4 %, respectively in the fifth, sixth and seventh runs (Fig. 6 C). The high removal efficiency of SMX and NOF suggested that continuous CQDs/Co@NC-5/PMS system could effectively remove antibiotics in the municipal secondary effluents.

Meanwhile, the dissolved cobalt concentration was very low during the continuous operation process. The above results demonstrated that CQDs/Co@NC-5/PMS system could effectively eliminate the antibiotics in the municipal secondary effluents. Therefore, the continuous CQDs/Co@NC-5/PMS system could be an alternative for the conventional advanced treatment process to selectively eliminate the antibiotics from the complex water matrices. In addition, considering the good stability of CQDs/Co@NC-5, the low temperature calcination could remove the adsorbed organic pollutants on the surface of CQDs/Co@NC-5, which could be a solution for the regeneration of CQDs/Co@NC-5. This requires further study to investigate the feasibility of the low temperature calcination

This study first prepared CQDs/Co@NC that possesses superior catalytic activity for PMS due to synergistic effect of confinement and photocatalysis. Furthermore, the CQDs doping could regulate the PMS activation pathway and the concentration of reactive species via controlling the visible light. The SMX degradation rate achieved 0.661 min−1 with the mineralization efficiency 46.1 % at 0.1 g/L of CQDs/Co@NC-5 and 0.4 mM of PMS. The toxicity of SMX solution decreased after the treatment of CQDs/Co@NC-5/PMS system. In the presence of visible light, the formation of radicals was promoted and the concentration of Co(IV) = O increased by 10 %. In general, the constructed system of CQDs/Co@CN-5/PMS showed superior catalytic activity for PMS and high resistance to water components, which provided an alternative system for the effective removal of emerging organic pollutants from the complex water matrices. Further research needs to focus on the regeneration of CQDs/Co@CN-5 and the economic analysis of CQDs/Co@CN-5/PMS system.

Shizong Wang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Jianlong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
